subject: Physics
unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Why is it difficult to walk on a very smooth icy surface?

options.A: There is too much gravitational force
options.B: There is very little friction between shoes and ice
options.C: Ice is too cold for walking
options.D: The normal force is zero on ice

questions.hint: Walking requires friction to push backward against the ground.
questions.explanation: Walking relies on static friction between shoes and ground. Ice reduces friction significantly, making it hard to push off. Option A is false; gravity is still present.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Why is it difficult to walk on a smooth surface?

explanations_tiered.definition: Static friction between shoe and ground provides the necessary grip to push off while walking.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Friction is required to generate forward force. Ice reduces friction, causing slipping.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Ice is slippery because there's not enough grip (friction) for your shoes to push against.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Low static friction on ice means your shoes cannot exert a backward force without slipping, so forward motion is difficult.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Walking requires static friction to provide horizontal impulse. On ice, \(f_{s,\text{max}} = \mu_s N\) is very small due to low \(\mu_s\).
reasoning_steps: Ice has very low coefficient of friction.
misconceptions: Some think gravity changes on ice — gravity is unchanged.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Walking needs friction. Ice → low friction → slipping.

glossary_entry.definition: Friction that prevents relative motion between surfaces at rest.

flashcard.back: Very low friction between shoes and ice.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Very low friction between shoes and ice.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Friction preventing motion between stationary surfaces.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Icy surface → low friction → hard to walk.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with rough surfaces that provide high friction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain role of friction in walking
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Low friction on ice makes walking difficult.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A student holds a heavy book at a constant height of 1 m for 5 minutes. How much work is done by the student on the book?

options.D: It depends on the mass of the book

questions.hint: Work requires displacement in the direction of the force.
questions.explanation: Work is \(W = F_{\parallel}S\). The force is upward, but displacement is zero (book not moving), so work = 0 J. Options B–D incorrectly assume effort implies work.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: What will be the work done by your hand if you hold your exercise book at 1 m height for two hours?

explanations_tiered.definition: Work requires displacement in the direction of the applied force.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Holding stationary requires force but no displacement → zero work in physics sense.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Even though your arm gets tired, the book doesn't move, so no work is done on it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Work = force × displacement in direction of force. Displacement = 0 → work = 0 J.
misconceptions: Confusing physiological effort with physics work (effort =/= work).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: No displacement → no work (even with force).

glossary_entry.definition: Force × displacement in direction of force.

flashcard.front: Hold book stationary at 1 m for 5 min. Work done?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Work done holding a stationary book?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast holding vs lifting: lifting does work, holding does not.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. No displacement → work = 0 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: If the speed of a ball is tripled, by what factor does its kinetic energy increase?
questions.hint: Kinetic energy depends on speed squared.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\). Tripling speed: \((3v)^2 = 9v^2\), so kinetic energy increases by factor 9. Option A confuses with linear relationship.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: For a fixed mass, if the speed of the ball is tripled, what will be its kinetic energy?

explanations_tiered.definition: Kinetic energy scales with the square of speed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Tripling speed increases kinetic energy by \(3^2 = 9\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Three times faster gives nine times the motion energy.
misconceptions: Linear thinking: triple speed → triple energy (incorrect).

glossary_entry.definition: Kinetic energy is proportional to speed squared.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine kinetic energy change from speed change

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A pump lifts \(200\text{kg}\) of water through a height of \(15\text{m}\) in \(10\text{s}\). What is its power? (Use \(g = 10\text{m/s}^2\))
questions.explanation: Work done against gravity \(W = mgh = 200\text{kg} \times 10\text{m/s}^2 \times 15\text{m} = 30000\text{J}\). Power \(P = W/t = 30000/10 = 3000\text{W}\). Option A forgets g or height.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: power of a water pump that can lift 500 kg water through 10 m in 5 s

explanations_tiered.definition: Power = work / time. Work against gravity = mgh.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: First compute work (energy transferred), then divide by time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A pump that lifts a heavy load quickly needs more power.

formula_analysis.name: Power for lifting
formula_analysis.derivation: From P = W/t and W = mgh

misconceptions: Using only mh (200×15=3000) forgets g → 300 W (option A).

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of work or energy transfer.

flashcard.front: 200 kg lifted 15 m in 10 s, g=10. Power?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 200 kg up 15 m in 10 s, g=10. Power?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to use g=10 if given.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: How fast must a \(2\text{kg}\) object move to have a kinetic energy of \(64\text{J}\)?

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: How fast must a 1 kg ball move to have kinetic energy of 50 J

explanations_tiered.beginner: To have 64 J of motion energy, a 2 kg object must move at 8 m/s.
misconceptions: Forgetting factor 2 gives \(v = \sqrt{64/2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66\) m/s (not an option).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A net force of \(15\text{N}\) acts on a \(3\text{kg}\) block. What is its acceleration?
questions.explanation: Newton's second law: \(a = F/m = 15\text{N} / 3\text{kg} = 5\text{m/s}^2\). Option A multiplies force and mass incorrectly.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: A force of 10N acts on a block of mass 2kg, acceleration = 5 m/s²

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration = net force / mass (Newton's second law).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A smaller mass accelerates more for the same force.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Newton's second law: \(\sum F = ma \Rightarrow a = \sum F / m\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Substitute F=15 N, m=3 kg.
misconceptions: Multiplying force by mass (15×3=45 m/s²) is a common error.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Check students divide force by mass correctly.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate acceleration from force and mass

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A bathroom scale shows 50 kg when a person stands on it. What does this reading actually represent?

options.B: The person's weight divided by g

questions.hint: Scales measure force but are calibrated to show mass on Earth.
questions.explanation: Scales measure normal force (weight), then divide by g (≈9.8) to display mass in kg. Option A is incorrect because scales actually measure force, not mass directly.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: What do the scales on the roadsides or in the bathroom actually measure? Your mass or your weight?

explanations_tiered.definition: Bathroom scales measure the downward force (weight) but are calibrated to show mass assuming Earth's gravity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Scales read force / g = mass. On the Moon, the same scale would show a smaller number.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The scale actually measures how hard you push down, then divides by 10 to show kg.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Scale reads normal force = weight (mg), then divides by g ≈ 9.8 to display mass in kg.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scale measures \(F_N = mg\). It displays \(m = F_N/g\). If g changes, reading changes even though mass constant.
reasoning_steps: Display divides weight by g to show mass-equivalent.
misconceptions: Some think scales measure mass directly — they measure force, then convert.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scale reads weight, displays mass (assuming Earth g).

flashcard.front: Bathroom scale shows 50 kg. What does it actually measure?
flashcard.back: Weight (force), then divides by g to show mass.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does a bathroom scale actually measure?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Weight (force), then divides by g to show mass.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain calibration: if g changes, reading changes but mass constant.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Scale reads weight, then divides by g to show mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: In an experiment, a wooden block is pushed with increasing force on a smooth surface. What happens to its acceleration?
questions.hint: Newton's second law: acceleration is directly proportional to net force.
questions.explanation: From \(F = ma\), if mass is constant, acceleration is directly proportional to net force. Doubling force doubles acceleration. Option A would happen if mass increased.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: When you exert force on a block, acceleration doubles if force doubles

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's second law: \(a = F_{\text{net}}/m\), so acceleration is proportional to net force for constant mass.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Doubling force doubles acceleration; tripling force triples acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Push harder → block speeds up faster.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Acceleration is directly proportional to net force when mass is constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: \(a = F/m\) ⇒ linear relationship between \(F\) and \(a\). Slope = \(1/m\).
reasoning_steps: Mass constant, force increases → acceleration increases proportionally.
misconceptions: Some think acceleration decreases with more force (confusing with air resistance).

glossary_entry.definition: One quantity increases linearly with another.

flashcard.front: Increase force on block (constant mass) → acceleration?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise direct proportion with constant mass.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Acceleration is directly proportional to net force (a = F/m).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A book rests on a table. Which pair of forces is an action-reaction pair according to Newton's third law?

options.A: Weight of book and normal force from table
options.B: Weight of book and gravitational force of book on Earth
options.C: Normal force on book and normal force on table
options.D: Weight of book and force of book on table

questions.hint: Action-reaction pairs act on different objects.
questions.explanation: Newton's third law: Earth pulls book down (weight), book pulls Earth up (equal and opposite). Option A are equal but act on same object (not action-reaction).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: action and reaction forces are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction and they act on different objects

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's third law: forces come in equal-magnitude, opposite-direction pairs acting on different objects.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Action-reaction: book pulls Earth; Earth pulls book. These act on different objects.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If Earth pulls the book down, the book pulls Earth up — that's an action-reaction pair.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Weight (Earth on book) and gravitational force of book on Earth are action-reaction. Normal force and weight act on same object, so not a pair.
explanations_tiered.advanced: \(\vec{F}_{\text{Earth on book}} = -\vec{F}_{\text{book on Earth}}\). Weight and normal force are not third-law pairs because they act on the same object.
reasoning_steps: Forces must be on different objects.
misconceptions: Many think weight and normal force are action-reaction — they are equal but act on the same object.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Action-reaction: different objects, equal magnitude, opposite direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Pair of equal and opposite forces acting on different objects.

flashcard.front: Action-reaction pair for book on table?
flashcard.back: Earth pulls book down; book pulls Earth up.

concept_map_nodes.target: Gravitational force of book on Earth

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Action-reaction pair for book on table?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Earth on book (weight) and book on Earth (gravitational).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: For every action, equal opposite reaction on different objects.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Clarify that normal force and weight are not third-law pairs.

concept_map_nodes.target: Gravitational pull of book on Earth

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Action-reaction pairs act on different objects: Earth-book gravitational pair.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
questions.hint: Vector quantities have both magnitude and direction.
questions.explanation: Force is a vector because it has magnitude and direction. Mass, work, and speed are scalars (no direction). Option C is the only vector.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Force is a vector quantity and is represented as \(\bar{F}\)

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector has both magnitude and direction; a scalar has only magnitude.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Force is a vector; mass, work, and speed are scalars.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Force has a direction — you push left or right — so it's a vector.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Force requires both how strong (magnitude) and which way (direction), so it is a vector.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Force transforms as a vector under coordinate rotations; it obeys vector addition (parallelogram law).
reasoning_steps: Force has magnitude and direction → vector.
misconceptions: Some think work is a vector — it's scalar (no direction).

flashcard.front: Which is a vector: mass, work, force, speed?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which is a vector: mass, work, force, speed?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Force has magnitude and direction; others are scalars.
